caveats: a: Public occupation data are broader than 325620.
a: Business-model mix drives large dispersion.
rho: Regulatory sources establish workflow requirements, not adoption rates.
rho: Generative outputs need qualified review.
e: The supplied firm count is margin-bridged and estimated.
e: FDA registration does not prove LMM size, independence, or external recurring revenue.
s5: Economy-wide demographic proxy.
s5: No public five-year transfer denominator for this code.
q: No public contract sample measures automation pass-through.
q: Owned-brand and contract-manufacturing economics differ sharply.
d5: Official sources define products and requirements but do not forecast real demand.
d5: Category growth and product lifecycles vary widely.
o: Operator share may migrate among domestic contract manufacturers, brands, and foreign suppliers.
o: This is not an employment-retention estimate.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B contract-manufacturing and private-label revenue, plus repeat wholesale sales of owned formulations, with formulation, compliance, testing, filling, and packaging support.
lens.deviation_from_default: The industry mixes consumer-brand product companies and outsourced contract manufacturers. The lens retains manufacturers with recurring external B2B production or repeat wholesale relationships and excludes brand-only marketing entities so operator economics remain coherent.
lens.excluded_activities: Brand-only marketers with no operating manufacturing service, captive plants, shell entities, distributors, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Independent LMM manufacturers and contract manufacturers that repeatedly formulate, blend, fill, package, and support perfumes, hair and skin preparations, lotions, sunscreens, and other cosmetics for external brands or channels.

a.bridge: BLS describes occupations for the broader NAICS 3256 and chemical manufacturing rather than six-digit 325620. I translate that mixed manufacturing occupation base into wage-weighted tasks: planning, customer service, artwork and label review, regulatory submissions, formulation search, marketing support, procurement, and quality documentation are exposed; physical compounding, filling, testing, warehousing, and maintenance are less exposed. The bridge may understate exposure in brand-heavy firms and overstate it in high-throughput contract plants.
caveats: Public occupation data are broader than 325620.
Business-model mix drives large dispersion.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted role and task data from 10-15 contract and branded manufacturers, including current automation by workflow, would materially revise the estimate.
a.rationale: The code contains document- and knowledge-intensive brand, formulation, regulatory, and customer work alongside physical manufacturing, yielding a moderate exposed share.

caveats: Official sources define products and requirements but do not forecast real demand.
Category growth and product lifecycles vary widely.
d5.change_evidence: Category-level five-year unit forecasts and retailer scanner data split by hair, skin, fragrance, sun care, and contract manufacturing would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Beauty and personal-care products are repeat-consumption physical goods, with innovation and channel expansion supporting volume. Household formation, premiumization, ingredient restrictions, SKU churn, and economic sensitivity create downside; the estimate is constant-price and excludes mix-led revenue inflation.

caveats: The supplied firm count is margin-bridged and estimated.
FDA registration does not prove LMM size, independence, or external recurring revenue.
e.change_evidence: Classify the injected 186-firm universe using FDA facility registrations/product listings, business databases, websites, and customer interviews to determine actual operating model and repeat external revenue.
e.rationale: Many LMM producers sell recurring formulation and manufacturing to external brands or recurring wholesale channels, but brand-only companies, captive facilities, fashion-driven single-product businesses, weak compliance systems, and concentrated customers fall outside or are unattractive under the lens.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 325620 ($4,366,580,729) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 325620 ($36,233,624,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1801 (margins.json[325]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Chemical (Specialty) EBITDA/Sales 18.01%); revenue/employee $675,484 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 325620); class <5: 449 firms, avg revenue/firm $677,699 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $122,054 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 162 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,165,537 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $390,013 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 130 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,669,469 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $840,971 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 186 firms, avg revenue/firm $17,503,263 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,152,338 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 87 firms, avg revenue/firm $58,084,402 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $10,461,001 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 44 firms, avg revenue/firm $605,966,795 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $109,134,620 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 186

caveats: Operator share may migrate among domestic contract manufacturers, brands, and foreign suppliers.
This is not an employment-retention estimate.
o.change_evidence: Brand make-versus-buy surveys, domestic capacity and import trends, and customer plans for insourcing or direct overseas sourcing would move this estimate.
o.rationale: Consumers still require physical products that must be formulated, blended, tested, filled, labeled, and released by an accountable manufacturer. Digital tools can alter discovery, design, and selling but cannot eliminate production; some brands may insource or source abroad.

caveats: No public contract sample measures automation pass-through.
Owned-brand and contract-manufacturing economics differ sharply.
q.change_evidence: Target-level contracts, bid histories, price-volume-mix bridges, gross margin by customer, and customer procurement interviews would directly estimate pass-through.
q.rationale: Contract manufacturing and private-label accounts often re-bid or negotiate at renewal, allowing customers to share savings. Proprietary formulations, regulatory reliability, switching friction, service levels, and owned brands support retention, but price-transparent channels and retailer power compress it.

caveats: Regulatory sources establish workflow requirements, not adoption rates.
Generative outputs need qualified review.
rho.change_evidence: Measured pilots across cosmetic product listing, label review, quality investigations, demand planning, and customer quoting at representative plants would move the range.
rho.rationale: Customer-service, document drafting, product-data management, demand planning, and creative variants can be deployed within five years, but safety substantiation, claims boundaries, formulation validation, data integration, and accountable quality decisions limit implementation.

s5.bridge: Federal Reserve owner-age chartbooks cover small businesses generally, not 325620 control transactions. I use owner age as succession pressure and discount for closures, family/internal succession, minority investments, and deals outside five years; strategic acquisitions by brands may push this industry above the all-business proxy, while founder dependence can prevent transfers.
caveats: Economy-wide demographic proxy.
No public five-year transfer denominator for this code.
s5.change_evidence: A denominator-based PitchBook/CapIQ/Grata study of 325620 control deals and closures since 2018, paired with owner-age and succession interviews, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: A large estimated population and active strategic logic support recurring control opportunities, but brand concentration, founder dependence, compliance diligence, and non-sale exits reduce the transferable fraction.

principal_driver: Repeat physical consumption plus document-intensive formulation and compliance workflows creates a usable AI opportunity.
principal_weakness: Contract repricing and extreme business-model heterogeneity can erode retention and comparability.

sources.supports: BLS includes Soap, Cleaning Compound, and Toilet Preparation Manufacturing (3256) in its industry overview and provides common-occupation context.
sources.title: Chemical Manufacturing: NAICS 325

sources.supports: Census defines the industry as preparing, blending, compounding, and packaging products including perfumes, shaving and hair preparations, creams, lotions, sunscreens, and cosmetics.
sources.title: NAICS 325620 Toilet Preparation Manufacturing

sources.supports: FDA describes Cosmetics Direct data entry, validation, and submission workflows for facility registration and cosmetic product listing.
sources.title: Registration & Listing of Cosmetic Product Facilities and Products

sources.supports: FDA states labeling is important to market entry and regulated under the FD&C Act and Fair Packaging and Labeling Act, while firms remain responsible for product safety.
sources.title: Cosmetics Labeling

sources.supports: FDA states cosmetics must be safe under labeled or customary use and that it can act when reliable information shows a product does not meet safety requirements.
sources.title: Product Testing of Cosmetics

sources.supports: Federal Reserve chartbooks include firm breakdowns by owner demographics and an age-of-owner chartbook, used as broad succession evidence.
sources.title: 2024 Firms in Focus chartbooks on small business data

title: Toilet Preparation Manufacturing
